currency.name: Argentine Peso
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 1,500 ARS (US$ are widely used too)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Argentina on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Argentina takes up the second-largest country on the continent, sitting in the long southern half 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Argentina?
mapQuestions.answer: Buenos Aires, on the Atlantic coast. About a third of all Argentines live in the wider Buenos Aires area.

mapQuestions.question: Which big mountain range divides Argentina from Chile?
mapQuestions.answer: The Andes — the longest mountain range in the world, running 7,000 km down South America's west coast.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Argentina?
mapQuestions.answer: Aconcagua, 6,961 metres, in the Andes near the Chilean border. It is the highest peak in the entire Western Hemisphere.

mapQuestions.question: Where is the southernmost city in the world?
mapQuestions.answer: Ushuaia, in Argentine Tierra del Fuego. The next stop south of Ushuaia is Antarctica.

phrases.english: A herbal tea drink (and a verb)

foods.name: Asado
foods.description: A long Argentine BBQ, ribs and steak grilled slowly over wood embers on a "parrilla" grill. Almost every Sunday lunch, all year, every region.

foods.name: Empanada
foods.description: A folded pastry with a filling inside (beef, ham and cheese, chicken, corn). Every Argentine province has its own version. The mendocina has olives and raisins; the salteña is small and spicy.

foods.name: Dulce de leche
foods.description: Caramel made by slowly boiling milk and sugar until it turns thick and golden. Argentines put it on everything: toast, ice cream, alfajores, pancakes, biscuits, cake.

foods.name: Alfajores
foods.description: Two soft buttery biscuits sandwiched together with dulce de leche and rolled in coconut, chocolate or sugar. The national snack.

foods.name: Choripán
foods.description: Grilled chorizo sausage in crusty bread, with chimichurri (a fresh herb-and-garlic sauce) drizzled over the top. Eaten at football stadiums, outside parks, after night markets.

foods.name: Alfajores
foods.description: Two soft shortbread biscuits sandwiched around a thick layer of dulce de leche, sometimes rolled in coconut. The classic Argentine sweet treat, found on every corner.

scavengerItems.label: Someone drinking mate from a gourd with a metal straw
A Lionel Messi or Argentina football jersey
Tango dancers in a square, or a tango mural on a wall
An asado (BBQ) firing up with ribs over wood embers
The Andes mountains in the distance
A penguin (in Patagonia they are everywhere on beaches)
Pampas grass blowing flat in the wind
A wide European-style boulevard or building (Buenos Aires)
The Casa Rosada (the pink presidential palace) or a brightly painted Boca house
A guanaco or llama on a mountainside
An Andean condor circling above (3-metre wingspan)
A glacier breaking off into a lake (Perito Moreno)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Called "the Paris of South America" for its wide boulevards, European architecture, cafés and tango clubs. Also the home city of Messi and Maradona.
The wild southern third of the country. Endless windy steppe, glacier-fed lakes, granite spires (like Mount Fitz Roy), and penguin colonies on the beach.
Shared with Brazil. A horseshoe of 275 waterfalls along 2.7 km of cliff. Wider, taller, and with more drops than Niagara Falls.
The wine capital, in the foothills of the Andes. Hundreds of vineyards, including some of the highest-altitude wine country in the world.
A Swiss-Alps-style mountain town in northern Patagonia. Wooden chalets, lake after lake, chocolate shops, and skiing in winter.
The "End of the World", the southernmost city on Earth. Most ships heading to Antarctica leave from here.

senses.smell: Wood smoke from an asado grill, leather, the bitter herb smell of mate, freshly baked alfajores, sea air at La Boca, eucalyptus on a country breeze…
senses.hear: A bandoneón playing tango, Argentine Spanish singing like Italian (especially the "ll" sound said as "sh"), "Che!", "Boludo!", a crowd chanting at a football match, the slurp of mate through a metal straw…
senses.taste: Smoky beef with chimichurri, sweet dulce de leche on everything, bitter mate tea passed around a circle, deep Malbec wine, salty crisp empanadas…
senses.feel: Cold wind sweeping across the pampas, soft worn leather of a gaucho saddle, the heat from a parrilla grill, fine Andean snow, summer humidity in Buenos Aires…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is football basically a religion in Argentina?
stories.body: Argentina has won the men's football World Cup three times (1978, 1986, and 2022), and basically nothing else makes the country more emotional. The country's two most-loved people are footballers: Lionel Messi, often called the greatest player ever, and Diego Maradona, who died in 2020 and is mourned like a saint. When Argentina won the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, over 5 million people poured into the streets of Buenos Aires to welcome the team home, in one of the biggest gatherings in human history. Maradona is so loved that there is an actual church for him, the "Iglesia Maradoniana", with thousands of members and its own version of Christmas (Maradona's birthday, 30 October).

stories.question: Where did tango come from?
stories.body: Tango is a passionate dance and music style that grew up in the working-class neighbourhoods of Buenos Aires and Montevideo (Uruguay) in the late 1800s. European immigrants, African ex-slaves and cowboys from the pampas (the gauchos) all brought their own music to the docks, and tango emerged from the mix. The dance has a close hold, sharp leg moves and pauses full of tension. UNESCO protects it as world heritage. Across Buenos Aires you can still find "milongas" — dance halls where ordinary people, some in their 80s, dance tango for hours every night of the week.

stories.question: Why do penguins live in Argentina?
stories.body: Argentina has thousands of kilometres of Atlantic coastline reaching all the way down to Antarctica. The cold ocean currents are full of fish, which makes the coast perfect for Magellanic penguins. They come ashore every spring to mate, lay eggs and raise chicks in huge colonies — Punta Tombo, on the central Argentine coast, hosts about a million penguins every year. You can walk a path with penguins waddling on either side. Argentina also has the Perito Moreno glacier in southern Patagonia, one of the few glaciers in the world that is still growing instead of shrinking, with huge chunks of ice crashing into the lake below every few minutes.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Penguins choose the same partner every year, often for life. What do you think makes that work for them?

stories.question: Why does Argentina look so European?
stories.body: Argentina took in more European immigrants per person than almost anywhere else on Earth. Between 1850 and 1950 around 7 million Europeans arrived, mostly Italian and Spanish, plus huge numbers from Germany, Wales, Ireland, France and Lebanon. About 60% of Argentines today have some Italian ancestry. That is why Buenos Aires has wide European boulevards and grand opera houses, why Argentine Spanish has an Italian-style singsong rhythm, why pasta and pizza are everyday foods, why Argentines wave their hands when they speak, and why "che" (the Argentine "hey, mate") sounds suspiciously like Italian. Pope Francis, whose family is Italian, is Argentine.

convoQuestions.question: Best Argentine food you have eaten so far?
Have you watched anyone drink mate? What did you make of it?
Have you heard Argentine Spanish? It sounds more Italian than Spanish — true?
If you could pick one — Messi memorabilia, tango lesson, or pampas horse ride — which one?
Argentina swings between super formal and super wild. What's been your favourite moment so far?
Argentina is famous for its writers (Borges), artists, and 5am footy matches. Which side of it surprised you?

wordSearch.hint: Argentine foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Gaucho horse
animals.description: On every estancia (cattle ranch) across the pampas. Often ridden by gauchos in flat hats.

animals.name: Aberdeen Angus cattle
animals.description: Black cows in vast herds — these become the famous Argentine asado beef.

animals.name: Magellanic penguin
animals.description: In Punta Tombo and Peninsula Valdés on the Patagonian Atlantic coast.

animals.name: Guanaco
animals.description: Wild relative of the llama. Patagonia, Tierra del Fuego, parts of the Andes.

animals.name: Andean condor
animals.description: One of the largest flying birds — 3m wingspan, soaring above the Andes.

animals.name: Southern right whale
animals.description: Peninsula Valdés in June–December. Mothers and calves come close to shore.
